question: Will there be a space station with artificial gravity by June 2026?
textDescription: This question resolves Yes if, on or before the close date, there is a space station which has artificial gravity (presumably to be achieved by rotation). The gravity must be at least 0.05g at the strongest, and the rotation of the station must have been intentionally effected, specifically for the purpose of creating artificial gravity.
newsShortReason: No space station with artificial gravity exists or is scheduled to exist by June 2026.
newsKeyEvidence: The International Space Station (ISS) continues to operate without artificial gravity, with no plans for such a feature. ([nasa.gov](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/spacestation/2026/02/05/dragon-preps-artificial-intelligence-and-medical-gear-fill-crews-day/?utm_source=openai)) | Russia patented a design for a space station with artificial gravity, but it is not expected to be operational by June 2026. ([daily-sun.com](https://www.daily-sun.com/20/848128?utm_source=openai)) | The Voyager Space Station, a proposed commercial space station with artificial gravity, is scheduled to begin construction in 2026, but it is not expected to be operational by June 2026. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_space_stations?utm_source=openai))

question: Will there be a space station with artificial gravity by June 2026?
textDescription: This question resolves Yes if, on or before the close date, there is a space station which has artificial gravity (presumably to be achieved by rotation). The gravity must be at least 0.05g at the strongest, and the rotation of the station must have been intentionally effected, specifically for the purpose of creating artificial gravity.
newsShortReason: No space station with artificial gravity is operational or planned by June 2026.
newsKeyEvidence: Aegis Station's Gradient One, a proposed artificial gravity testbed, is scheduled for 2035, not June 2026. ([aegisstation.com](https://aegisstation.com/gradientone.html?utm_source=openai)) | A Russian patent for a space station with partial artificial gravity was registered in January 2026, indicating early conceptual work without imminent implementation. ([nasa.gov](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/spacestation/2026/04/16/station-orbits-higher-as-crew-runs-new-science-experiments/?utm_source=openai))
